content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901        322-6441            322-6444

May 24, 1979

A-79-135

James Rodgers
184 Smithwood
Milpitas, CA 95035

Dear Mr. Rodgers:

This letter is in response to your request for advice concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act as they relate to your duties as a Milpitas City Councilman.1/ We hope that the following discussion is helpful in assisting you to determine which decisions create some potential problems under the Act. If you need further advice with respect to any particular decision, please do not hesitate to contact us. The information set forth below was provided by you and your attorney, Mr. Sal Spano, in conversations held on March 7, 1979, and April 17, 1979. The facts as we understand them are as follows.

You are an employee of Milpitas Materials, which is in the business of supplying concrete. Milpitas Materials is wholly owned by Jon Minis; you do not have any ownership interest in this company. Although you are corporate vice president of Milpitas Materials, you do not have any role in the management of the business; all management decisions are made by Mr. Minis alone. Your duties include taking orders from customers, dispatching (both tasks which are also performed by Mr. Minis), and outside customer service (a task performed solely by you). Although a minor part of the job is to build

_____________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. References to the Act mean the Political Reform Act of 1974.

Mr. James Rodgers
May 24, 1979

Page Two

sales, this does not include a duty to do so by encouraging
growth and development in general. Approximately 90% of all of
Milpitas Materials' business is with its "regular customers"
who do not solicit bids from Milpitas Materials but who instead
directly order concrete for small bids such as driveways,
patios and swimming pools.

Milpitas Materials receives bid work in either of two
ways. First, Mr. Minis may decide to place bids on projects
listed in the Daily Pacific, a contracting newspaper which
lists the jobs up for bid and the general contractors who are
preparing to bid for them. After acquiring the job-specifications, Mr. Minis determines his bid and sends it to all of the
general contractors who are preparing bids for the entire job.
In the second kind of bid work, "private works", the general
contractor and subcontractors are selected without bidding.
Thereafter, the subcontractor approaches the materials supplier
and solicits a bid from the supplier for the materials. The
two situations are quite different with respect to the knowledge a materials supplier has of who will actually be doing
the work. In the first process, the materials supplier sends
his bid to several contractors and does not know which one will
be selected. In the second process, the supplier knows which
subcontractor will be doing the work since the subcontractor
only solicits bids once he has obtained a contract. An example
of a contractor who operates in this second manner is Robert's
Construction Company, to whom Milpitas Materials supplies concrete
for four to eight projects per year. Although bid work constitutes only 10% of Milpitas Materials' total sales, because of
the size of projects it builds, Robert's Construction Company,
owned by Robert Houret, is one of Milpitas Materials' largest
customer accounts.

In the geographical area in which it operates, Milpitas
Materials has approximately 10 to 15% of the market (that is,
it supplies approximately that amount of all the concrete
supplied). This is consistent with the fact that Milpitas
Materials has approximately 10% of the batching and trucking
capacity in the area. This supply area extends from Saratoga
in the west to Fremont in the north. Only a small amount,
approximately 5%, of all the work Milpitas Materials does is
actually within the city limits of Milpitas.

_______________________

2/ Occasionally this process is slightly different. In
the case of public buildings for example, general contractors
are required to pick the subcontractors they will be using prior
to bidding. Therefore, Milpitas Materials would submit its bid
to the subcontractors instead of the general contractors.

Mr. James Rodgers
May 24, 1979

Page Three

You are a member of the City Council of Milpitas. The
City Council is called upon from time to time to make decisions
concerning land use planning and the course of development in
the city. Through such decisions, the City Council controls
the amount of new construction in Milpitas and can, therefore,
significantly affect businesses in the construction industry
in the city. You have asked us the extent to which, given the
facts cited above, you can participate in decisions and remain
in compliance with the Political Reform Act. The following
analysis is intended to give you some general guidelines. You
should examine the facts surrounding each individual decision
in the context of these guidelines in order to determine whether
you should disqualify yourself.

Section 87100 of the Act provides that:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Section 87103 provides that:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on:

***
(c) Any source of income***aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by
or promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is made;
or

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a[n]***officer [or] employee***

These sections mean that while you are an officer or are
employed by Milpitas Materials, and if you were to leave its
employ, for 12 months after your departure, you cannot participate
in any decisions of the City Council which will have a fore-
seeable material financial effect on Milpitas Materials if
the effect is distinguishable from the effects of the decision
on the public generally.

Mr. James Rodgers
May 24, 1979

Page Four

The Commission has also adopted a regulation which specifies
when the effects of a decision are "material" under the Act.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. This regulation contains
monetary guidelines addressed to predictable changes in assets,
income, market value, etc. of an official's financial interests
as well as general standards. Two of these standards provide
that the effect of a governmental decision on an official's
source of income is material if the governmental decision will
directly affect the amount of income to be received by the
official or if there is a nexus between the governmental decision
and the purpose for which the official receives income. Under
the first of these two standards, you should not participate in
any decision which will foreseeably lead to either an increase
or decrease in the amount of income you receive from Milpitas
Materials. Under the second standard, you should not participate
in any governmental decision which will accomplish something which
your private employer pays you to do. In other words, if part
of your duties for Milpitas Materials were to encourage growth
and spur construction in order to increase the market for build-
ing supplies, you should not, as a member of the City Council of
Milpitas, participate in decisions which have that result as a
foreseeable effect.

Under the other standards provided in the regulation, you
should not participate in governmental decisions which will
foreseeably have one of the specific effects enumerated in the
regulation on Milpitas Materials' assets, liabilities, annualized
gross revenues or annualized net income, or which will otherwise
foreseeably have a substantial financial effect on Milpitas
Materials.

The issue of the foreseeability of an effect was addressed
by the Commission in the opinion requested by Tom Thorner,
1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, 1975). Although this opinion
does not articulate any one standard, it is clear that a fore-
seeable result is something more than a possible result; there
must be some likelihood or substantial probability that the
result will occur. In the Thorner opinion, the Commission
addressed various fact situations involving bidding on supplying
materials to construction projects. As only a negligible amount
of Milpitas Materials' business is bid work, these standards are
of little help in most situations you will be facing. For those
few situations in which they will be applicable, you should use
them with the understanding that they are conclusions that were
reached in the fact situations presented in that case and they

Mr. James Rodgers
May 24, 1979                                Page Five

are useful only to the extent that you face a parallel situation.3/

In general, you should be prudent with respect to City
Council decisions concerning whether construction requiring a
substantial amount of concrete will be permitted. In the face
of such a decision, you must use your knowledge of Milpitas
Materials' customers and the operations of the developer or
contractor who is before the Council to determine whether it
is likely that the decision will generate business for Milpitas
Materials. If, based on the information available to you, it
is reasonably foreseeable that business will be generated for
Milpitas Materials by virtue of the decision, you must disqualify
yourself... In addition, you should be prudent with respect to
any decision which involves persons who account for a large
share of Milpitas Materials' business on some kind of predict-
able basis. In such situations, you should again use your
knowledge of the person involved to assess whether a decision
on your part which was negative to the customer's interest
would cause the customer to retaliate by taking his business
away from Milpitas Materials. If you have any reason to believe
that that is the case, you should disqualify yourself.

After consideration of all these factors, however, if you
have no reason to think that a decision will have an affect on
Milpitas Materials' business (or, of course, on any other finan-
cial interests you may have), the Act does not require you to
disqualify yourself from participating in the decision. Moreover,
the propriety of your participation under the Act does not change
even if Milpitas Materials, through circumstances not foreseeable
at the time the decision was made, later does profit as a result
of it. The ultimate test is the foreseeability of the effect
at the time the decision was made.


3/ I note that the term "regular customer" is used in one
of the situations in Thorner. That term was not used in the opin-
ion as a term of art, as you were using it in describing certain
of Milpitas Materials' customers. The fact, therefore, that a
decision before you involves or does not involve one of your
"regular customers" should not be accorded undue significance.

Mr. James Rodgers
May 24, 1979                                                     Page Six


     If you have any further questions or would like our help
with respect to any particular decision you face, please do
not hesitate to call us.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division


STCima
cc: Mr. Sal Spano
